assertion: Applying the same ordered sequence of ActionEnvelope frames to a fresh state on two independent implementations produces byte-for-byte identical final state.
notes: Core property: pure reducer + identical action stream => identical state. Grounded in ActionEnvelope as the canonical state-update unit. The conformance/README.md documents this as the invariant the existing FullHandshake test already checks. Part 2 will run fast-check with arbitrary valid action streams.

assertion: After replaying the same action stream starting from the same Snapshot, client-side state equals host-side state.
notes: Extends the determinism invariant to the cross-impl scenario: client starts from Snapshot.fromSeq, applies subsequent ActionEnvelope stream, and converges with host. The conformance/README explicitly names byte-for-byte convergence as the acceptance criterion.

assertion: In a single connected session, successive ActionEnvelope frames deliver strictly increasing serverSeq values.
notes: Property: serverSeq is a global sequence counter. Clients depend on monotonicity for reconnect replay (lastSeenServerSeq). A fuzz run should generate arbitrary action streams and verify no out-of-order serverSeq ever appears in a well-behaved host.

assertion: Host responds with a JSON-RPC ParseError (-32700) and does not crash or hang when it receives a WebSocket frame containing truncated/incomplete JSON.
notes: Malformed-frame class: truncated JSON. The standard JSON-RPC error code -32700 (ParseError) is the required response. Part 2 will fuzz with bytes-cut-at-arbitrary-offsets.

assertion: Host responds with InvalidParams (-32602) and does not crash or hang when a required field carries the wrong JSON type (e.g. protocolVersions as a string instead of an array).
notes: Malformed-frame class: wrong-typed field. Derived from the schema's required typed fields. Part 2 generates every required field with a mutated type (string->number, array->object, etc.).

assertion: Host responds with InvalidParams (-32602) and does not crash or hang when a frame omits any schema-required field.
notes: Malformed-frame class: missing required field. One row covers all required-field violations. Part 2 will enumerate every required field in InitializeParams, DispatchActionParams, etc. and drop each one independently.

assertion: Host responds with InvalidParams (-32602) when an action or reconnect result frame omits its discriminator field (e.g. no 'type' field on a ReconnectResult).
notes: Malformed-frame class: missing union discriminator. The reconnect result uses a 'type' discriminant (Replay vs Snapshot). Actions use 'type' discriminants. Missing discriminator is a key fuzz class.

assertion: Host gracefully rejects (or ignores with a defined response) an ActionEnvelope whose action.type is not in the known action-type enum, without crashing or hanging.
notes: Malformed-frame class: unknown action variant. Derived from the discriminated union in StateAction. A fuzz run sends an action with type='unknown-fabricated-type'. The D2 spec rules (soft assertNever pattern) suggest clients should tolerate unknown variants; host behavior for unknown-action dispatch is a gap.

assertion: Host returns a ReconnectSnapshotResult (not Replay) when lastSeenServerSeq is negative, zero, or beyond the replay buffer.
notes: Malformed-frame class: out-of-range/negative serverSeq. lastSeenServerSeq is typed 'number' in the schema; a fuzz run sends -1, 0, MAX_SAFE_INTEGER. The schema does not declare a minimum — the host MUST handle gracefully. The Snapshot fallback is the expected safe response.

assertion: Client reducer applying the same serverSeq twice (duplicate frame) produces the same final state as applying it once OR raises a detectable error — it must never silently corrupt state.
notes: Malformed-frame class: duplicate frames. The schema does not define behaviour for duplicate serverSeq delivery. This is a property gap: clients should be idempotent on replay or reject the duplicate. Part 2 fuzz inserts duplicate envelopes into a valid stream.

citation.excerpt: "description": "Protocol versions the client is willing to speak, ordered from most\npreferred to least preferred.

assertion: Host returns UnsupportedProtocolVersion (-32005) with supportedVersions data when no entry in protocolVersions is acceptable.
notes: Property: for any input set of protocol versions that excludes the server's supported set, the host MUST return -32005 with structured data. Fuzz dimension: arbitrary semver strings in protocolVersions.

assertion: Host returns a well-defined error (UnsupportedProtocolVersion -32005 or InvalidParams -32602) when protocolVersions is an empty array.
notes: Edge case of the version-negotiation property. An empty array matches no server version. The schema does not declare minItems on protocolVersions — this is an under-specified edge.

assertion: A valid action stream applied in serverSeq order produces a final state that all conforming implementations agree on, regardless of the delivery order of prior independent actions.
notes: Property: serverSeq is the total ordering. Any shuffle of actions with the same serverSeq assignment (if the server ever assigned duplicates, which it must not) is a correctness bug. Part 2 stress-tests concurrent action delivery by shuffling frames within a small window and asserting that re-sorted replay converges.

assertion: Host responds with ParseError (-32700) and does not crash or hang when it receives a WebSocket frame containing arbitrary non-JSON bytes (binary garbage, null bytes, lone surrogates, etc.).
notes: Malformed-frame class: non-JSON bytes. Broadest fuzz class. Part 2 fuzzes with: random bytes, valid UTF-8 but non-JSON, valid JSON but not an object (bare string, array, null). All require ParseError response and no crash.

citation.excerpt: "description": "Reconnect result when the server can replay from the requested sequence.\n\nThe server MUST include all replayed data in the response."

assertion: After reconnecting with lastSeenServerSeq=N, applying the replayed actions on top of the pre-disconnect state produces a final state identical to a client that never disconnected.
notes: Property: replay convergence. The MUST in the description grounds this as a normative invariant. Part 2 will generate random sequences, simulate disconnect after K frames, reconnect, verify convergence.

assertion: All ActionEnvelope frames received after a Snapshot carry serverSeq > Snapshot.fromSeq — never equal to or less than fromSeq.
notes: Property: fromSeq establishes the lower bound for subsequent envelopes. Violation means a client would double-apply an action. Fuzz dimension: replay an action with serverSeq == fromSeq after snapshot delivery.

assertion: Host does not crash or hang when receiving an extremely large WebSocket frame (e.g. megabytes of repeated valid JSON). It either processes it or closes the connection with a defined WebSocket close code.
notes: Malformed-frame class: oversized frames. The schema does not define a message-size limit; this is a robustness property. Part 2 sends frames of 1MB, 10MB, 100MB and observes whether the host returns a defined error or cleanly closes the connection.

assertion: Host gracefully rejects or ignores an ActionEnvelope whose channel URI targets a resource the client is not subscribed to, without corrupting state.
notes: Property: channel routing invariant. Fuzz dimension: send a session-channel action with a fabricated session URI the client never subscribed to. Spec does not define the rejection mechanism explicitly.

citation.excerpt:
"description": "Verifies that the AHP connection is still alive and keeps it from being
closed by idle-timeout intermediaries (proxies, load balancers, etc.).

The server MUST respond regardless of whether the client has completed
`initialize` or holds any subscriptions.

assertion: Host responds to a ping request even if initialize has not yet been sent.
notes: Property: ping is connection-level, not session-level. The MUST in the description grounds this. Fuzz dimension: send ping as the very first message, before initialize.

assertion: Applying zero actions to a Snapshot produces a final state equal to the snapshot's state — the reducer is identity on the empty stream.
notes: Trivial but load-bearing property: identity on empty stream. Part 2 verifies this as a baseline before adding actions. If this fails, the reducer initialisation is broken.
